at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Filing fee
at_a_glance.detail: Flat fee for all small claims cases

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Trial de novo in circuit court

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If a claim exceeds the $10,000 cap, you cannot split it into multiple small claims cases; you must file in a higher court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Wisconsin allows you 6 years to file claims on written contracts and 6 years on oral contracts.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $2,000, and they failed to repay you as promised.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Gather any evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You lent $5,000 to a colleague with the understanding it would be repaid in a year, but they haven't paid you back.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide documentation of the loan agreement and any repayment attempts.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $8,000 and failed to make the agreed payments.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided consulting services worth $3,500, invoiced the client, but they haven't paid.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring copies of the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $6,000 on their account with your business.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $4,500 in unpaid rent over the past three months.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease agreement, rent ledger, and any notices sent to the tenant.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for goods with a $1,200 check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the original check, bank notice of insufficient funds, and any communication with the issuer.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer liability for defaulted loan
claims.example: You co-signed a $9,000 loan for a friend who defaulted, and now the lender is seeking payment from you.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the loan agreement and any correspondence from the lender.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Wisconsin provides avenues for consumers to seek redress for various disputes, with specific statutes governing deceptive trade practices and lemon laws.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $2,000 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged you $1,500 but failed to perform the agreed-upon tasks.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the service agreement, payment proof, and evidence of inadequate service.

claims.name: Dispute over auto repair charges
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $3,000 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and refuses to refund or correct the work.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the repair invoice, payment proof, and any communication with the mechanic.

claims.name: Contractor failed to complete work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $7,500 to renovate your bathroom, but they abandoned the project halfway through.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the contract, payment records, and evidence of incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver goods/services
claims.example: You paid $2,500 for catering services for an event, but the caterer never showed up.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the contract, payment proof, and any communication with the vendor.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month gym membership, but they continued to charge your account for several months.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring cancellation confirmation, bank statements, and any communication with the gym.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $9,000 that broke down within a week, and the dealer refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the sales contract, payment proof, and any communication with the dealer.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A retailer advertised a product for $500, but charged you $700 at checkout without honoring the advertised price.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the advertisement, receipt, and any communication with the retailer.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Wisconsin allows you 6 years to file claims for property damage.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver hit your parked car, causing $4,000 in damages, and refuses to pay for repairs.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Provide police reports, repair estimates, and any communication with the other driver.

claims.name: Neighbor caused damage to your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell onto your fence during a storm, causing $2,500 in damages.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Bring photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left your rental unit with $3,000 in damages beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Provide the lease agreement, move-in/move-out inspection reports, and repair estimates.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone else's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and destroyed $1,500 worth of landscaping.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Bring photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left your bicycle with a friend for safekeeping, and it was returned with $800 in damages.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Provide evidence of the property's condition before and after, and any communication with the custodian.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Wisconsin provides specific statutes governing landlord-tenant relationships, including security deposits and habitability standards.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord withheld your $1,200 security deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Bring the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Security deposit deductions not itemized
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $500 from your security deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Provide the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had severe mold issues, making it uninhabitable, and the landlord refused to address the problem.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Bring photos of the conditions, repair requests, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord wrongfully retained tenant's property
claims.example: After eviction, your landlord kept your personal belongings worth $2,000 without legal justification.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Provide evidence of the property, its value, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, effectively evicting you without following legal procedures.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Bring the lease agreement, evidence of the lock change, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent after lease termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent upfront, but after terminating the lease early, the landlord refused to return the unused portion.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Provide the lease agreement, payment records, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant missed three months of rent payments totaling $3,600 and has since vacated the property.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Bring the lease agreement, rent ledger, and any notices sent to the tenant.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You paid $500 to fix a leaking roof after the landlord failed to address it, and deducted the cost from your rent.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Provide repair receipts, photos of the issue, and any communication with the landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wages
claim_categories.blurb: Wisconsin provides specific statutes governing wage payments and employer obligations.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 for hours worked but has not issued payment.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Bring pay stubs, timesheets, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer refused to issue your final paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Provide resignation notice, pay stubs, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in sales commissions, but your employer has not paid them.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Bring commission agreements, sales records, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime last month, but your employer paid only regular wages.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Provide timesheets, pay stubs, and any communication with the employer.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Wisconsin allows you 3 years to file claims for personal injury and 6 years for property damage.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on an unmarked wet floor in a store, resulting in a sprained ankle and $1,200 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from incident. Bring medical records, photos of the hazard, and any communication with the store.

claims.name: Damage to loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your camera worth $800, and they returned it with significant damage.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Provide evidence of the property's condition before and after, and any communication with the borrower.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your bicycle worth $600 without permission and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Bring proof of ownership, police reports, and any communication with the neighbor.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: Wisconsin provides specific statutes governing vehicle-related disputes, including auto repairs and towing.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's charges
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $2,500 for repairs you didn't authorize and refuses to refund the amount.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Provide the repair invoice, payment proof, and any communication with the mechanic.

claims.name: Vehicle wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and the towing company charged you $300 for its return.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Bring photos of the parking spot, towing receipt, and any communication with the towing company.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction actions are handled in small claims court regardless of the amount claimed.
Disputes involving the title to real estate are not handled in small claims court.
Defamation claims are not suitable for small claims court due to their complexity.
Professional malpractice claims exceed the jurisdictional limits of small claims court.
Matters such as divorce, child custody, and probate are not handled in small claims court.
Small claims court does not have jurisdiction over claims against the federal government.
Workers' compensation claims are handled by the Department of Workforce Development.
Class action lawsuits are not suitable for small claims court.
Small claims court cannot grant injunctions or order specific performance.
Federal claims are outside the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Disputes subject to mandatory arbitration clauses are not handled in small claims court.
Claims against deceased individuals must be filed in probate court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in small claims court; however, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees may be recoverable in certain cases as specified by statute.

where_to_file.court_name: Small Claims Division of the Circuit Court

filing_options: Any county where ONE defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially incurring additional costs and delays.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing is available and encouraged for small claims cases.

service_fees.notes: Varies by county; contact local sheriff's office for exact fees.
Varies by county; contact local clerk's office for exact fees.

forms.name: Petition for Waiver of Fees and Costs

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and service fees.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees are added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fee and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy stays.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: A 30-day written notice is required before pursuing statutory penalties for a bad check.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific demand requirement for wage claims, but written demand may strengthen the case.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of damages within 21 days of lease termination when withholding security deposits.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: No specific pre-filing requirements for consumer protection claims.

forms.name: Summons and Complaint (Small Claims)
forms.description: To initiate a small claims action.

forms.name: Summons and Complaint (Small Claims) with Instructions
forms.description: To initiate a small claims action with instructions for self-represented parties.

forms.name: Order for Dismissal Non-Eviction (Small Claims)
forms.description: For the court to dismiss a small claims non-eviction action and to record the terms of the stipulation for dismissal.

forms.name: Declaration Based on Noncompliance for Money Judgment Non-Eviction (Small Claims)
forms.description: To request the court to enter a money judgment based upon noncompliance.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff or constable personally hands the papers to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail by clerk
methods.description: Clerk sends the papers via certified mail to the defendant.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A private individual serves the papers to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with the laws of the state where the defendant is served.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be found, service by publication may be permitted with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is avoiding service, you may request alternative service methods from the court.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer but must appear at the scheduled hearing.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant doesn't appear, the court may grant a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Reopen Small Claims Judgment

response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, the defendant may appeal within 15 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If the counterclaim exceeds the small claims limit, the case may be transferred to circuit court.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys but are not required to be.
hearing.format: Bench trial, typically 10-15 minutes per case.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter from the court clerk as soon as possible before the hearing.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within 30 days.
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some counties offer mediation services on-site; check with the local court.

appeals.type_notes: The appeal is a new trial in the circuit court.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed within 15 days.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a garnishment action, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor is employed and earns above the exemption threshold.
exemptions: 80% of disposable earnings or 30 times the federal minimum wage per week, whichever is greater.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution, serve it on the bank, and the bank freezes and turns over funds to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account.
exemptions: $5,000 of deposits are exempt from garnishment.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property.
methods.description: File the judgment with the county clerk to place a lien on the debtor's property, which must be satisfied upon sale or refinancing.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real property and intends to sell or refinance.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt property.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution, have the sheriff seize non-exempt property, and sell it to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable non-exempt property.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before it expires; no specific form, but must be filed with the court.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged in bankruptcy.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among judgment creditors is generally determined by the order in which liens are filed.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses may appear without an attorney in small claims court.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a trade name must file a Fictitious Name Statement.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may authorize an employee to appear on their behalf.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face limitations in enforcing contracts in court.

county_variations.detail: Milwaukee County requires e-filing for small claims cases.
Dane County offers mandatory mediation for small claims disputes.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect defendant information
pitfalls.what_happens: Suing the wrong party or using incorrect information can result in dismissal or unenforceable judgments.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the defendant's legal name and address before filing.

pitfalls.title: Missing statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will lead to dismissal of the case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Confirm the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type before filing.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to properly serve the defendant can delay the case or result in dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow the court's rules for service of process carefully.

pitfalls.title: Insufficient evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of evidence can lead to losing the case, even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements before the hearing.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring court deadlines
pitfalls.what_happens: Missing deadlines can result in default judgments or dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Keep track of all court dates and deadlines, and respond promptly.

pitfalls.title: Overlooking counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: Failing to file a counterclaim may waive your right to seek damages related to the same dispute.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Consider any potential counterclaims and file them within the required timeframe.

recent_changes.title: Increase in small claims filing fee
recent_changes.description: The filing fee for small claims cases increased from $94.50 to $99.50.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in small claims court. Many individuals represent themselves successfully.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: The typical timeline for a small claims case is between 30 to 90 days from filing to resolution.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: You can sue for up to $10,000 in small claims court.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may grant a default judgment in your favor.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims judgment within 15 days.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment is valid for 20 years and can be renewed before it expires.

state_specific_notes: Wisconsin allows e-filing for small claims cases in most counties.
Mediation services are available in some counties to help resolve disputes before trial.
Small claims court can handle eviction cases regardless of the amount claimed.
Statutory penalties may apply for writing bad checks, including double the amount of the check.
Landlords must return security deposits within 21 days of lease termination, providing an itemized list of any deductions.

interesting_findings: Wisconsin's small claims court handles a wide range of cases, including evictions and replevins.
The state provides a comprehensive self-help law center for individuals representing themselves in small claims court.
E-filing is encouraged and available for small claims cases, streamlining the filing process.
